So back on that story, I use fingernails to express something. example, there's a movie called Ferris Buell's Day Off. And there's a great scene in the movie where the teacher, I can't remember the guy's name, he's a great actor, he's very dry and he's actually being all monotone in front of the class. The Holly Smoot Tariff Act, which anyone raised or lowered raised tariffs in an effort to collect. He's like, okay class, open up your book, you know, we're going to talk about the Smoot Holly Act. And as he's talking in this unbelievably monotone, meaning one tonality, and it's going to sort of same pace, you watch the kids like to start nodding off, one kid is drooling. And when we see that, we laugh because we know it's funny because we've all have been on the wrong end of someone like that who's boring and his tone less. Essentially, no matter how great their message might be, we don't hear it. We tune out. One of the things about the human nervous system, the way it's built, the way we move through the world, is we tend to habituate very quickly on a stimulus that is the same. So in other words, if I'm sitting here right now, yes, the air is moving onto my skin. And when I first walked in the room, I was actually aware that it's a little bit cold in here. But now, within five minutes, my skin no longer even feels that it's still cold, but I habituated. So my nervous system blocks it out so I can focus on other information that's more important and more relevant to what's going on here. See, our conscious mind only has a limited amount of processing power. While our unconscious mind has massive, unlimited processing power. So what we do is we block out the stuff that's unimportant. Well, guess what? If you try to talk to someone and you have really important information that can change their lives, but you do it in a way that causes them to habituate, meaning you're tone-less or you're almost tone-less and you just sort of drag on, people will tune you out and you can't get your point across at all. That's an example of having awful, like, the worst tonality, right? As was in that movie, right? But then that's just the continuum. That's like the worst tonality possible. And let's say there's someone like me who's really mastered the art of tonality, where I know how to, like, you know, sound enthusiastic whether I really am or not. And I can tell you anything like it's a secret or, you know, move my body and get people interested and have some of these really, these amazing competencies that we're going to discuss, these core competencies, which are knowledge and impact, enthusiasm, you know, which I'm sure you're going to get to. But so if I represent one end of the continuum, which is unbelievable tonality, and then the teacher from Ferris-Buelau represents terrible tonality, then we have everybody who's watching this, so all you are watching and learning here, they're somewhere in between. You know, some people are really naturally great with tonality and others are monotone. So the thing I would say about tonality, interesting part, is that no matter how bad your tonality might be, you, you, it doesn't really matter. At some point in your life, you've used every tonality that is required to become a master of the straight line. In other words, there's only 10 tonalities that you have to learn to essentially muster at will, right? And we'll get into them a little bit later in this training here, but the point is this, you don't have to learn how to use them for the first time in your life. You've already used them, but you use them by accident, use them randomly when you actually felt a certain way. There was once in your life where you did feel absolutely certain, so of course you talked in a tone of absolute certainty. There was a time where you felt really sincere and empathetic and you were able to talk in the tone of sincerity. There was a time when you felt that you were being really, really reasonable and you looked at someone and said, you know, I'm reasonable, you're reasonable. Isn't that fair enough? Come on, honestly. And you had the tone of the ultimate reasonable man, right? So those are some of the key tones we need to become influencers and we've all used them from time to time. The goal of this module, if there were any one goal, it would be to take what you've done unconsciously, accidentally, make it conscious and make you do it or allow you to do it on purpose, to essentially show you how to muster the right tonalities and show you where precisely you need to use them in a presentation. And once you get to that point, what it does for you in terms of strengthening your impact and getting people to perceive you in the right way, the results are literally mind boggling. So that's my take on tonality, what it really is. So why don't we start off, I guess, with some of the competencies here that really are important when it comes to tonality. When it comes to my mind, let's say the first one to me is something I call enthusiasm. Essentially, the ability, and it's an ability, by the way, to sound enthusiastic about something, even if you're not that enthusiastic. Now, let me explain. I'm not trying to tell you that you should sound enthusiastic as a manipulative measure towards someone like, let me just act like I'm enthusiastic so people will buy from me. What I'm saying is this, chances off you're selling something that you even believe in remotely, that when you first heard about it, you probably were enthusiastic, but you yourself habituated that enthusiasm has waned. But the problem is the person that you're presenting to is hearing about the information or hearing about your product for the first time. So imagine now you're trying to transfer this enthusiasm to someone else who's hearing it for the first time, but you already habituated, so you're like, I'm not that enthusiastic. So you start talking in a normal tone because to you it's all that. But to them, actually, it's something they can get enthusiastic about. So that's why you need to actually be able to recreate that original enthusiasm that you first had when you heard about something, to be able to impart that to another human being. Does that make sense to you? Absolutely. So what I've done myself, as I've learned, basically, how to, you know, you could wake me up out of a dead sleep and say, can you sound enthusiastic? And I'd say, let me just tell you, and I can sound enthusiastic about like raindrops. Like these raindrops are the best raindrops you've ever seen. I am telling, whether it's a, well, it could be whale meat, it could be, you know, it could be the butcher, the baker, the can, the butcher, the baker, you don't know about the butcher, the baker, the can. Let me just tell you, and I'm like, you're like, wow, what? And you automatically, what happens? You key into me. You're like, what? What do you have? Because what happens is when we see someone who's enthusiastic, the natural connection, the irresistible connection that we make is that we say to ourselves, well, geez, if he's that enthusiastic, it must be good. That's what it really translates to to another person. Will they buy simply because you're enthusiastic? Well, hopefully they're a bit more sophisticated than that, but it's the enthusiasm that essentially opens them up to saying, wow, I want to know more. That's the power. So whether it's, you know, any tonality that that that one compensates to be enthusiastic to me is just absolutely crucial. Let me give you one more, then you can give me a couple you want to discuss. Another one is impact. And what I mean by impact is the ability through tonality to instantly capture someone's attention from the first moment that you open up your mouth to when they first lay eyes on you through body language, through proper tonality, to have that irresistible impact with someone says, wow, this is a person who's worth listening to. This is like something about, and they just, you know, they don't know what it is. They can't quite define it. They couldn't put it into words, so to speak, but they just know that you've, bam, you've made an impression. You don't make an impression in the first five or six seconds through the words that you say. They, it happens far too quickly for that over time. Yeah, you'll make the words you say matter a lot, but in the first few seconds of an encounter, impact is made by tonality and it's made by body language. Okay, so we're going to go through what are those, what are the tonalities that create impact and what are the body language is that also create impact and also what are the ones that destroy impact then, you know, sort of make you essentially get tuned out by the person.
